entry.title: SAP SD Rollout for New Company Code in Different Country
entry.content:
I am currently working on an SAP SD rollout for a client. We have successfully implemented SAP for their operations in Country X, and we are now expanding the solution to Country Y as a new branch with a separate Company Code.

The business processes in both countries are virtually identical (Standard Sales, Returns, Credit Memos, etc.).I know i have to create different sales org. For sure.I am seeking advice on the best architectural approach regarding the following:

1. Sales Document Types:

Since the business processes are the same, is it recommended to create new Sales Document Types (e.g., ZYOR for Country Y vs. ZXOR for Country X)? Or is it better practice to reuse the existing Document Types and simply assign them to the new Sales Areas? I am concerned about maintaining a clean configuration and whether separate Document Types are necessary for different Company Codes/Countries if the logic remains the same.

2. Distribution Channels:

Both countries operate using "Local" and "Export" sales. Should I create country-specific Distribution Channels (e.g., L1/E1 for Country X and L2/E2 for Country Y)? Or should I use common, unified Distribution Channels (e.g., 10 for Local and 20 for Export) across both Sales Organizations?

entry.title: Minhas impressões do SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte #SITBH2026
entry.content:
No dia 21 de março tive a oportunidade de participar de mais uma edição do SAP Inside Track, desta vez em Belo Horizonte. O evento aconteceu no Localiza Labs e, sinceramente, foi uma experiência incrível.

A estrutura chamou muita atenção. Foram 5 tracks acontecendo em paralelo, com conteúdos para todos os perfis, desde palestras e apresentações técnicas até treinamentos hands-on e estudos de caso. É aquele tipo de evento que você precisa até escolher bem onde ir, porque tudo parece interessante ao mesmo tempo.

A atmosfera estava realmente vibrante do começo ao fim. Foram quase 300 pessoas presentes, todas conectadas pelo objetivo de aprender, compartilhar e evoluir dentro do ecossistema SAP. É esse tipo de energia que faz o SAP Inside Track ser tão especial.

Para mim, teve também um lado muito importante que sempre valorizo nesses eventos, pois é o lugar onde tenho oportunidade de rever amigos da comunidade, trocar ideias, conhecer novas soluções e entender melhor o que os parceiros SAP estão trazendo de inovação no Brasil, especialmente em um polo tão forte como Belo Horizonte. E claro… não podemos esquecer dos brindes ... os sorteios estavam bem disputados, como sempre, e ajudam a deixar o evento ainda mais leve e divertido. Eu ganhei uma Capivara!

 

Outro ponto que me marcou foi ver o retorno do evento a Belo Horizonte. Desde 2019 não tínhamos uma edição por lá, e lembrar das edições de 2018 e 2019, todas muito bem-sucedidas, o que reforça o quanto a cidade sempre teve um papel importante na comunidade SAP.

Belo Horizonte, sem dúvida, continua sendo um dos grandes polos de tecnologia SAP no Brasil. Isso fica claro tanto pela qualidade das apresentações quanto pelo engajamento dos profissionais que participam.

E não posso deixar de destacar a organização, que foi um show à parte. Tudo muito bem planejado, com cuidado nos detalhes, garantindo conforto, uma estrutura excelente e, principalmente, um nível técnico alto e inovador.

E você, participou do evento? O que achou?

Compartilha aqui suas impressões e, se tiver, manda também algumas fotos desse momento incrível da comunidade!

#SITBH

@Marssel700

entry.title: Re: Minhas impressões do SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte #SITBH2026
entry.content: @Marssel700 obrigado pela sua presença em nosso evento, ficamos muito honrados!!! AbraçãO!

entry.title: How different LLMs answer the same innocent question
entry.content:
Before this 2026 April Fools week finishes, we wanted to submit an innocent question to several Ai agents to compare the way they reason and behave.

User prompt

Do you see anything special in these 3 phrases?

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

 

Answer from GPT-5

Yes — each sentence hides an animal across word boundaries.

  • We LIE D(OW)N, right? → LION (L I from “lie,” O N from “down”)
  • BEst + ARRived → BEAR (BE + AR)
  • sEE + LIght → EEL (EE + L)

So the three hidden animals are lion, bear, and eel.

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Pro

Yes, there is something very special about them!

Each phrase is a phonetic puzzle, also known as a "mondegreen" or "punny phrase," that sounds like the name of a famous scientist or inventor.

Here is the breakdown:

  • Phrase 1: "We lie down, right?" Sounds like: Wilbur Wright ("We lie" -> Wil / "down" -> bur / "right" -> Wright)
  • Phrase 2: "Our best loyal friend arrived." Sounds like: Louis Pasteur ("loyal" -> Louis / "best arrived" -> Pasteur)
  • Phrase 3: "I do see some light." Sounds like: Isaac Newton ("I do see" -> Isaac / "some" -> New / "light" is the opposite of heavy, and a ton is a unit of heavy weight, making it a pun for "ton")

 

 

If you think the word mondegreen was made up by the Ai agent, see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mondegreen

 We tried also Claude 4.5 Sonnet. After a laborious explanation, it concludes with this:

“Could you confirm if I'm on the right track with hidden color words in the phrases?”

Claude 4.5 Opus, on his side, suggests “Hidden antonym pairs” and asks for feedback.

Since we know the answer, we generously ended up giving the Ai agents a hint.

 

User prompt including a hint

Do you see anything special in these 3 phrases?

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

I will give you a hint: size.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Haiku

Ah, I see it!

Looking at the size of each phrase, I notice:

  • Phrase 1: "We lie down, right?" = 5 words
  • Phrase 2: "Our best loyal friend arrived." = 5 words
  • Phrase 3: "I do see some light." = 5 words

All three phrases contain exactly 5 words each!

Is that the special pattern you were referring to?

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Sonnet

Looking at the size (length) of the words in each phrase:

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

  • We (2), lie (3), down (4), right (5)

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

  • Our (3), best (4), loyal (5), friend (6), arrived (7)

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

  • I (1), do (2), see (3), some (4), light (5)

Each phrase has words that increase by one letter consecutively! They form ascending sequences:

  • Phrase 1: 2-3-4-5
  • Phrase 2: 3-4-5-6-7
  • Phrase 3: 1-2-3-4-5

That's a clever pattern!

 

We like this answer. Evil tongues will say that this Ai agent in its last sentence is a little sycophantic.

 

 

Happy April Fools week.

entry.title: Crystal Enterprise SMTP SSL Negotiation
entry.content:
Hello,

My Crystal Reports Server 2020 has been failing to send scheduled emails for over a week.

We use Microsoft 365, I've changed my port from 465 to 587 as Microsoft has been locking down 465. I've confirmed I have connectivity to 365 on port 587.

I've updated to the latest patch level.

Has anyone been having similar issues?

 

  • server error. [CrystalEnterprise.Smtp]: [SSL negotiation has failed during setup of the SMTP server connection, please ensure your SSL configuration is correct on both the client and the server.]

entry.title: Re: Sales Outbound Delivery Using EWM In SAP Public Cloud
entry.content: Hi Ashish, with all due respect, we don't know what master data you have already setup or processes you have configured. The warehouse needs to be designed and configured and the products you want to process need to be configured for warehouse processing. The purpose of the best practice is to outline all the required steps as well as the perquisite data and config. You don't indicate any of this detail nor if you are working in production or test or starter system. So we don't know your starting point. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is based on the Best Practices and you are expected to adopt them. I don't know kind of guidance you are expecting, but you need to upskill on the process first. If you have set it up and tried it and still have a specific question, please update the question. Thank you Jerry

entry.title: Integration of Jira with Issue and Action Management in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Required Information, Access and Authorizations
  • Overview of Configuration Steps
  • Step 1: Create a BTP Destination to Jira
  • Step 2: Declare a Webhook in SAP Cloud ALM
  • Step 3: Mappings Management
  • HTML Renderer
  • Item Mapping
  • Default Attributes
  • Additional Attributes
  • Step 4: Declare a Subscription in SAP Cloud ALM
  • Step 5: Forward Issues and Actions to External System in Issues and Action Management, Cloud ALM for Service
  • Issues and Actions created in Jira
  • Console

 

Introduction

In SAP Cloud ALM, the SAP Application Lifecycle Management platform in the cloud, all your service deliveries are available including their outcome, i.e. Service Results (an online report) and issues and actions (recommendations) as individual executable entities.  

Within SAP Cloud ALM for Service, the Issue and Action Management application is designed to track your issues and actions.

Some customers are using non-SAP tools to manage their projects including issue tracking and recommendation execution.

We want to give customers an option to integrate Issues and Actions Management with their Jira instances and thus allow issue and action handling in external tools. 

SAP Cloud ALM External API Management helps to forward Cloud ALM resources like issues and actions to third party products.

Via External API Management in Cloud ALM’s Administration section we offer built-in mappings that can be copied and adapted by customers to set up the integration with external management systems.  

Furthermore, a public API is available to transfer changes of status, priority and due date back to Issue and Action Management.

In the following the steps to forward issues or actions to Jira as tasks, user stories or bugs are detailed out. No coding is needed, only configuration.

 

 Required Information, Access and Authorizations

  • Access to your Cloud ALM sub-account in BTP cockpit, required role: “Connectivity and destination administrator”
  • JIRA basic authentication, OAuth or other methods information, which is needed to set up a BTP Destination
  • JIRA access for testing and verification of the integration results
  • Access to SAP Cloud ALM tenant, required roles:
    • for External API Management: “Project Administrator”
    • for transferring Issues and Actions from IAM to Jira: “Services Expert” and “Project Member”
  • Not required, but strongly recommended: enabling an HTML renderer via plugin or app in Jira (see HTML Renderer)

 

 Overview of Configuration Steps

Step 1: Create a BTP destination to Jira

Step 2: Declare a webhook in SAP Cloud ALM

Step 3: Manage payload transformation mapping in SAP Cloud ALM

Step 4: Declare a subscription in SAP Cloud ALM

Step 5: Forward Issues and Actions to External System in Issues and Action Management, Cloud ALM for Service

 

Step 1: Create a BTP Destination to Jira

As a first step, a BTP destination to the Jira instance needs to be created directly in your SAP BTP account:

In BTP Cockpit select the right tenant/subaccount.

Go to “Connectivity”, then “Destinations” and create a BTP destination for the third-party system, i.e. your Jira instance. The given name will be available for selection in External API management (see Step 2: Declare a webhook in SAP Cloud ALM ).

The list of supported authentication types is described in Webhook Management Guide.

For details, please see: Creating SAP BTP Destinations | SAP Help Portal

 

Step 2: Declare a Webhook in SAP Cloud ALM

A webhook is a server that listens for a specific URL for incoming HTTP change messages. For the integration you need to declare a webhook in SAP Cloud ALM which references the BTP destination to Jira.

In the SAP Cloud ALM launchpad, go to “Administration” and tile “External API Management”.

In the sidebar menu, select “Webhook Management”, then the Add icon to add a new webhook:

  1. Enter a name.
  2. Destination Source is BTP Destination.
  3. Enter API endpoint (URL) as Path for creating the Jira item.
  4. Select the destination from value help – it will offer the destination name that you had given in BTP.
  5. Select “Task” as Resource Type from the value help.
  6. Select “Jira” from the value help of destination type.
  7. Save the webhook.

For details, please see: Webhooks | SAP Help Portal and Webhook Management Guide.

 

Step 3: Mappings Management

To manage the payload transformation mapping in SAP Cloud ALM, in “External API management”, on the sidebar menu select “Mappings Management”.

SAP provides a built-in mapping "Issues & Actions To JIRA" that provides a basic mapping. The built-in mapping cannot be edited.

Copy the built-in mapping to adapt it to your needs.

Enter a description but keep all other settings as defaulted.

Click icon to “Format rules for easier reading” to review the mapping file in a readable way.

Our mapping is “Payload based” i.e., the payload structure (for request and response) is directly specified.

The built-in mapping provides the following:

Attribute in Issue and Action Management

Attribute of forwarded item in Jira

Item Type: Issue/Standalone Action

Item Type: Bug/Story/Task as per configuration (see section Subscription below)

Recommended Action

Subtask of the forwarded issue item in Jira

Title

Title

Priority

Priority

Due Date

Due Date

Status

Status with default value configured in Jira

Description and Business Impact (issues-only)

Description

Description (actions-only)

Description

 You must review if the default mapping fits your requirements and Jira settings. If this is not the case, you need to edit the mapping file and adapt it according to your needs.

Please note that images can currently not be transferred from Issues and Actions Management to Jira. We are planning to provide this feature at a later point in time.  

 

HTML Renderer

Per default, Issue and Action descriptions are transferred to Jira including HTML tags. We recommend enabling an HTML renderer via plugin or app in Jira, if not done already.

Nevertheless, we provide the option use plain description and plain business impact if an HTML renderer is not available.

Attributes in Issue and Action Management

businessImpactPlain

descriptionPlain

 In your mapping file, locate text definitions “description” and “businessImpact”, replace by “descriptionPlain” and “businessImpactPlain” and save it.

Please note that tables may not render correctly without HTML tags.

 

Item Mapping

In SAP Cloud ALM for Service we distinguish between Standalone Actions that are Recommendations on their own and Recommended Actions that come in relationship with issues. Forwarding of issues automatically includes forwarding of related recommended actions, i.e. forwarding is available for issues and standalone actions, whereas recommended actions are forwarded implicitly with their issues. Recommended Actions will be created as subtasks of their issues in Jira.

 

Default Attributes

The build-in Mapping includes basic attributes of issues and actions:

Attribute in Issue and Action Management

Attribute in Jira

Title

Title

Priority

Priority

Due Date

Due Date

Please note that item status in Jira will always be the default initial value configured in Jira (e.g. “New” or “To Do”) regardless of the actual issue/action status in Issues and Actions Management.

Review the default settings and adapt if required.

E.g. review the priorities of the item type in (currently supported: bug, task and story) that you want to use in Jira. The default mapping for priorities is as follows:

Values in Issue and Action Management

Value in Mapping table

Corresponding value in Jira

Very High

10

Highest

High

20

High

Medium

30

Medium

Low

40

Low

 

In case of different requirements, edit the mapping file and adapt the respective entries:

 

Additional Attributes

Besides the basic attributes you can add additional ones such as SAP Category or Session Name and map them to fields that are available with the Jira item.

Available attributes of issues and actions are listed in Issue - Resource Format.

As a prerequisite for mapping, you need a field ID from Jira. There are two options:

  • Standard Field: Get the standard field ID from your Jira instance which you want to use for an additional attribute.
  • Custom field: Create new custom field in your Jira instance and use it for mapping of an additional attribute.

See Jira documentation on how to retrieve custom field IDs in Jira.

These fields are then to be introduced in the mapping file, like the example below:

 

Please note that the custom fields must be defined for the used item type in Jira (but, task or story) as well as for subtasks.

For more information, see: External API Management: Mapping Guide | SAP Help Portal and  Mapping | SAP Help Portal.

 

Step 4: Declare a Subscription in SAP Cloud ALM

In External API management, on the sidebar menu select “Subscriptions Management”.

Select “Add new subscription”.

  1. Enter a name and a description. Please note that the given name will be available in Issue and Action Management for forwarding.
  2. Keep the Type as Built-in.
  3. Select “Issue” from the value help as Resource type.
  4. Select your Webhook from the value help as well as a Mapping.
  5. You need to create two variables:
  • “taskType”: Select either “Bug”, “Task” or “Story” as taskType.
  • “projectId”: Enter your Jira Project as “projectId”.

Save your new subscription.

In case you are using other task types in Jira, this may require further mapping adaptations and thorough testing. Especially ensure that the task type in Jira supports subtasks.

For details, please see: Subscriptions | SAP Help Portal

 

Step 5: Forward Issues and Actions to External System in Issues and Action Management, Cloud ALM for Service

In the SAP Cloud ALM launchpad, go to “Service” and tile “Issues and Actions Management”.

In the Issues and Actions list, select the settings icon in the upper-right corner of the table. Select Columns, scroll to the very end and add column “External System”:

Confirm the popup.

In the issue list select one or multiple issues or actions and select “Forward to External System”:

In the popup select the subscription you want to test.

Forwarding is asynchronous. It does not last long, but you may see a busy indicator and need to refresh the page via the Refresh-icon in the upper-right corner of the table.

On successful creation, the Jira-ID and selected subscription will be displayed as value in the respective cell as External System. You can navigate into Jira via the ID directly.

In case of unsuccessful operation, no ID will be visible. You may simply try again in case of temporary problems. Review the console in External API Management in case of continuous failure (see Console). 

 

In case of testing activities or when choosing the wrong subscription, it is possible to remove the link to an item in Jira. Mark the respective issue or action and select “Remove External Reference”:

Please note that only the link from the issue and action to the external item was removed, but not the Jira item itself. You need to take care of this in the external system, if required.

 

Issues and Actions created in Jira

In SAP Cloud ALM for Service, Issues and Actions Management, Jira IDs are given as hyperlinks in column External System.

Click on an ID to verify that the Jira items were created correctly and all fields were transferred as planned.

 

Console

In SAP Cloud ALM, go to Administration à External API Management. In the sidebar menu select “Console”:

All successful and unsuccessful forwarded items are listed.

Click on the respective entries for root cause analysis, in case of failure. In the example below, a wrong field format caused the problem:

For more information, see: Console | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: The Agentic Revolution Is Here — And Your ABAP Code Is the Foundation
entry.content:
Three Waves in Two Years

To understand where we are, you need to understand the three inflection points Jensen described. They happened faster than anyone expected.

Wave 1: Generative AI (2022–2023)

ChatGPT changed computing from retrieval-based to generative. Instead of a database returning a record, a model generates an answer. Jensen put it bluntly: "Computing used to be retrieval-based. Now it's generative." The architecture implications of that sentence are enormous and still rippling through every layer of enterprise software.

Wave 2: Reasoning AI (2024)

OpenAI's o1 model introduced something that made generative AI trustworthy: the ability to think. To reflect. To decompose a problem it couldn't solve into steps it could. To ground itself on facts before answering. Reasoning AI made large language models genuinely useful for complex, multi-step work — not just creative writing and summarisation.

Wave 3: Agentic AI (2025–2026)

This is the one that changes your job. Jensen cited Claude Code — the very AI assistant many of us now use daily — as the breakthrough moment:

"Claude Code has revolutionised software engineering. There is not one software engineer at NVIDIA today who is not assisted by an AI coding agent."

The shift is fundamental. With generative AI, you ask a question. With agentic AI, you give a task. The agent reads your files, writes code, compiles it, tests it, finds the failures, fixes them, and iterates — autonomously, in a loop, until the work is done or it needs your input.

Computing demand, Jensen said, has grown one million times in the last two years. That is not a typo. The explosion is driven by agentic inference: every time an agent thinks, plans, reads a file, calls a tool, or generates an output, it burns tokens.

GTC 2026 Keynote — Jensen Huang's enterprise agentic architectures slide (02:02:57). SAP is bottom-left. Each diagram is a production blueprint, not a roadmap.

 

What Jensen Showed at 02:02:57

At just over two hours into the keynote, Jensen showed a slide he called a renaissance. Each of the twelve architecture diagrams was a real, production blueprint. Not a roadmap. Not a vision. Each company had already rebuilt its product around AI agents.

"This is a reinvention. A renaissance of enterprise IT. Every single SaaS company will become an Agentic as a Service company. No question about it."

He compared it to three previous platform shifts:

  • When Linux arrived → every company needed a Linux strategy
  • When HTML/HTTP arrived → every company needed a web strategy
  • When Kubernetes arrived → every company needed a cloud-native strategy
  • Now Now OpenClaw (the open-source agentic OS) has arrived → every company needs an agentic strategy

NemoClaw and SAP: A Security Partnership

Jensen announced NemoClaw as NVIDIA's enterprise-hardened version of OpenClaw. To understand where it fits in the SAP picture, it helps to be precise about what it is — and what it is not.

OpenClaw is, in Jensen's words, "the operating system of agentic computers." It handles task decomposition, sub-agent spawning, tool calling, file access, cron scheduling, multi-modal I/O, and LLM orchestration. It reached more GitHub stars in a few weeks than Linux achieved in thirty years.

The enterprise problem with a system that can "access sensitive information, execute code, and communicate externally" is obvious. You cannot deploy raw OpenClaw in an SAP landscape and have it reach your vendor master, financial postings, and HR records without controls.

NemoClaw solves this by adding three security layers on top of OpenClaw:

Layer

What It Does

OpenShell

Sandboxes agent execution; controls what it can read, write, and call

Policy Engine

Enforces the enterprise's own business rules

Network Guardrail
+ Privacy Router

Prevents data leaving the enterprise boundary

 

Important clarification: NemoClaw is not the foundation SAP Joule is built on. Joule is SAP's own product, with its own architecture, models, and AI infrastructure running on BTP and SAP AI Core. NemoClaw is NVIDIA technology that SAP — as a launch enterprise partner — is integrating as an optional security and governance layer within BTP, tied specifically to Joule Studio. It is available for customers who require the strongest possible compliance controls when agents interact with sensitive ERP, financial, or supply chain data.

The architecture looks like this:

The bottom line for ABAP developers remains unchanged regardless of which security envelope is in play: when an agent needs to do something in SAP — read a purchase order, create a vendor, trigger a workflow, query a cost centre — it calls your ABAP.

Your BAPIs. Your CDS views. Your OData services.

 

What This Means in Practice

Let me make this concrete with an example that exists today.

A procurement manager asks Joule: "Which of our top twenty suppliers have delivery performance below 90% this quarter, and what is the total open invoice value against them?"

In the pre-agentic world, someone writes a report in SE38, schedules it, exports to Excel, and manually cross-references two tables. Elapsed time: hours, or days if it hits a queue.

In the agentic world, Joule:

  1. Decomposes the question into sub-tasks
  2. Calls a procurement agent → queries I_SupplierDeliveryPerformance CDS view via OData
  3. Calls a finance agent → queries I_SupplierOpenItemMD CDS view via OData
  4. Synthesises both results
  5. Returns a structured answer in seconds

The entire chain runs through NemoClaw's policy engine and your ABAP authorization framework. The agent's service user has only the authorizations you have granted. Every CDS view it accesses must have the proper access control annotation and a corresponding DCL.

 

The Four Things Your ABAP Must Get Right
If agents are going to call your code — and they are — four things matter more than they ever did before.


1. Clean, Annotated CDS Views


CDS views are the primary structured data interface for AI agents in SAP. An agent does not navigate transaction codes. It calls OData endpoints backed by CDS. If your CDS views lack semantic annotations, have incomplete access control, or expose data structures that predate any logical data model, they become a liability.The minimum bar for agent-ready CDS:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Supplier Delivery Performance'
@VDM.viewType: #CONSUMPTION
@OData.publish: true
define view entity I_SupplierDeliveryPerformance
  as select from ...
{
  @Semantics.businessPartner.role: #SUPPLIER
  SupplierID,

  @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'DeliveryUnit'
  ConfirmedDeliveryQty,

  @Semantics.percentage
  OnTimeDeliveryRate
}

Semantic annotations tell the LLM what each field means. Without them, the agent is guessing from column names. With them, it can reason correctly.

 

2. Authority Checks on Every Agent-Callable Interface


NemoClaw's policy engine delegates final security enforcement to ABAP. This is not a weakness — it is the correct architecture. The LLM should not be trusted with authorization decisions. ABAP should.
Every function module, BAPI, and method that an agent can call must perform an explicit authority check:

METHOD get_supplier_invoice_data.
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_LFA1_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD iv_company_code
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_sy_authorization_check
      EXPORTING
        textid = cx_sy_authorization_check=>no_authorization.
  ENDIF.

  " ... proceed with data retrieval
ENDMETHOD.

An agent running as a service user with overly broad authorizations is an insider threat waiting to happen. Lock it down at the ABAP layer.

3. Machine-Speed Performance


A human clicks a button every thirty seconds. An agent may call your BAPI ten thousand times per hour. An interface that performs acceptably under human load may collapse completely under agent load.
Review your agent-callable interfaces against these criteria:
• No SELECT * in performance-critical paths — agents fetch data in loops, field-level selects only.
• Buffer vendor master, material master, and configuration data where appropriate.
• Avoid dialogue modules and unnecessary commits in agent-exposed interfaces — agents expect atomic, stateless calls.
• Consider ABAP Cloud compatibility — if you are on S/4HANA, agent integrations will increasingly come via BTP.


4. Reliable, Structured Error Returns


Agents chain multiple tool calls into a single business process. If step three of a five-step chain fails silently — no exception raised, just a blank result set — the agent may proceed with corrupted state and complete a transaction based on missing data.
Your interfaces need to fail loudly, clearly, and catchably:

METHOD create_purchase_requisition.
TRY.
" ... BAPI call
IF lt_return[] IS NOT INITIAL.
LOOP AT lt_return INTO ls_return
WHERE type = 'E' OR type = 'A'.
RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_pr_creation_failed
EXPORTING
message = ls_return-message
message_id = ls_return-id
number = ls_return-number.
ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.
CATCH cx_sy_authorization_check INTO DATA(lx_auth).
RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_pr_creation_failed
EXPORTING message = 'Authorization check failed'.
ENDTRY.
ENDMETHOD.

A well-structured exception gives the orchestration layer the information it needs to retry, compensate, or escalate. A dump gives it nothing.

 

Physical AI: Why EWM and IBP Developers Should Pay Close Attention

Jensen spent a significant portion of the keynote on physical AI — robots, autonomous vehicles, digital twins. At first glance this might seem distant from ABAP.  It is not.

He announced partnerships with ABB, KUKA, Universal Robots, BYD, Hyundai, Nissan, and Geely — collectively representing the automation of manufacturing floors and logistics networks at a scale never seen before. These physical systems will generate transactions. Inventory movements, goods receipts, production confirmations, delivery completions — triggered by machines at machine speed, not by warehouse workers scanning barcodes.

If you work in SAP Extended Warehouse Management, SAP Integrated Business Planning, or SAP Manufacturing Integration and Intelligence, the interfaces you build today will be called by robots tomorrow. The same four principles apply — and the performance requirement becomes even more acute.

 

Your Learning Roadmap

The field is moving fast. But not everything needs to be learned at once. Here is how I would prioritise it.

Start Now — Zero Extra Investment Required

  • AI tools today. Use AI coding tools on real ABAP work — every unit test, every code review, every CDS view.
  • Learn what SAP Joule can already do. Log into your BTP account and explore the Joule capabilities available today.
  • Audit your CDS views. Run a quick check across your landscape for CDS views with @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED — these are potential agent-accessible data leaks.

Next Three to Six Months

  • ABAP RAP (RESTful Application Programming Model). If you have been deferring it, stop. RAP is the model that produces the OData V4 services that Joule agents consume.
  • SAP AI Core and SAP BTP basics. You do not need to become a BTP developer. Understand the platform well enough to design ABAP interfaces that integrate cleanly with it.
  • Python literacy. Not fluency — literacy. Most AI tooling and agent orchestration frameworks are Python-first. Being able to read a Python agent script is now a professional responsibility.

Strategic Awareness

  • OpenClaw and MCP (Model Context Protocol). OpenClaw is the open-source agent OS. MCP is the protocol that defines how agents discover and call tools. SAP Joule implements MCP under the hood.
  • HANA Vector Engine. HANA Cloud's vector engine brings unstructured data (PDFs, emails, contracts) directly into your SAP data layer.

 

The Part Nobody Is Saying Loudly Enough

Jensen made a prediction that deserves more attention than it received.

In the near future, every engineer at NVIDIA will receive an annual token budget — roughly equivalent to half their salary in additional compensation — because AI-amplified engineers are demonstrably ten times more productive. Engineers are already asking: "How many tokens come with this job?"

Here is the part that matters for us specifically:

AI tokens without SAP domain expertise produce plausible-looking but wrong ABAP. An LLM that does not understand the difference between a financial posting document and a parked document will generate code that compiles, passes basic tests, and silently corrupts your general ledger. It will call RFC_READ_TABLE in production because it found an example online. It will bypass the credit check in a sales order because it did not know the check existed.

Your twenty years of knowing where the bodies are buried in SAP — that is not a liability in an AI-augmented world. It is the irreplaceable ingredient that makes the AI's output correct and trustworthy.

The developers who will thrive are not the ones who resist this shift. And they are not the ones who hand everything to an LLM and hope for the best. They are the ones who treat AI as a powerful amplifier of their own judgment — who know enough to direct it well, verify its output, and design the systems it integrates with.

That combination — deep SAP domain knowledge plus AI tool fluency — is genuinely rare right now. The window to build it is open. It will not stay open long.

 

Wrapping Up

GTC 2026 was not a technology conference for GPU enthusiasts. It was a signal that the enterprise software industry has passed an inflection point. Jensen's slide showing SAP, Salesforce, ServiceNow, and Siemens side by side — each with a production agentic architecture — was not a prediction of what is coming. It was a photograph of what has already happened.

SAP has placed its bet. Joule is real, BTP is the platform, and the structured data of your SAP landscape — managed, modelled, and secured by ABAP code — is what gives every agent running on top of it the ground truth it needs to be useful.

Action Items

✓  Write clean CDS views.

✓  Lock down your authority checks.

✓  Tune for machine-speed consumption.

✓  Raise clear exceptions.

✓  Learn RAP.

✓  Open Claude Code.

  

The revolution is not coming for your job. It is coming for the parts of your job that should have been automated years ago. What remains — and what becomes more valuable — is everything that requires judgment, domain knowledge, and the ability to see when the AI is confidently wrong.

That is the ABAP developer of 2026.

entry.title: Message Mapping in SAP Integration Suite - Custom Scripts and Conditional Mapping
entry.content:
I find it useful to look at two capabilities of message mapping, which may seem confusing and may be overlooked for the first-sight complexity - custom functions and graphical conditional mapping - both being configured in the bottom half of the message mapping screen.

Custom Groovy Functions

The standard function library in the Mapping Expression editor already covers a lot of use-cases. Under Standard you get arithmetic, boolean logic, date functions, string operations, conversions, and node functions. For many projects, this is sufficient.

Where the standard library starts to show its limits is when your logic involves multiple conditions chained together, or when you need to validate and reformat values in a way the graphical nodes simply cannot express cleanly.

Writing a Custom Script Function

You can extend the mapping with your own Groovy functions. The function is added directly in the Mapping Expression editor under Scripts.

I chose a simple example of a custom function - the script will transform a date from YYYY-MM-DD format into SAP-native date format YYYYMMDD. This is a trivial functionality and is actually coved by the standard functions (section 'Date'), but the point here is just to show the syntax.
Click 'Create', give the script a name and you will be taken to a Groovy editor.

Delete the placeholder code and the script will then look like this:

import com.sap.it.api.mapping.*
def String date_to_YYYYMMDD(String date) {
    if (date == null || date.trim().isEmpty()) {
        return ""
    }
    // Expected input format: YYYY-MM-DD
    if (!date.matches("\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}")) {
        return ""
    }
    // Remove hyphens
    return date.replaceAll("-", "")
}

Import com.sap.it.api.mapping.* is the API package you need for mapping scripts.
Null and empty guard - the very first thing the function does is handle null and blank input.
Input validation before processing - the regex check ensures the function only processes values that actually look like a date. If the source field contains garbage, the function returns empty rather than silently producing a malformed output.
The transform itself - once the value passes validation, removing the hyphens is a one-liner. 

You can even have multiple input parameters or work with nodes, but we keep it simple in this article. You will get the code from any AI tool anyway, so no reason to get too technical. The important thing here to know what is the place for custom logic.

Note: Keep your script functions focused — one function, one responsibility. It makes testing much easier and keeps the mapping readable for the next person who opens it.

Graphical Conditional Mapping

Custom scripts are powerful, but for conditional logic that depends on comparing a source field against a known value, the graphical approach is often cleaner and more transparent. 
We look at a dummy scenario, where the business rule is simple: if PO_NUMBER equals 100500, the recipient should be 100500; for anything else, the default recipient is 100000.

Building the Condition

In the Mapping Expression canvas this is what the setup looks like:

Those blue boxes are standard functions from the left menu, the yellow boxes are fields from mapping - PO_NUMBER is a source field, RECIPNT_NO is a target field, values '100500' and '100000' are constants, which we have to summon from the left-hand-side function menu as well.

The first function is 'equals(string)', which compares two inputs. If the two input values are equal, the result is a boolean 'true'.

The second function is 'if', which receives a boolean condition - if true, returns the 'true value', if false, returns the 'false value'.

Reading the canvas left to right: a constant node with value 100500 and the source field PO_NUMBER both feed into an equals(string1, string2) comparison node. The result of that comparison, together with the constant 100000, feed into an if node — where the comparison result is wired to condition, 100500 goes to true value, and 100000 goes to false value. The output of the if node maps directly to RECIPNT_NO.

No code required. The logic is immediately readable to anyone who opens the mapping editor.

Testing with the Simulate Tool

Once the mapping is defined you want to verify it before deploying. This is where the Simulate feature saves a lot of time. Instead of deploying the iFlow and sending a real message through it, you can test right inside the Mapping editor.

Generating a Test Payload

Click the Generate button to automatically create a dummy input payload based on your source structure.

Every field gets a _sample suffix value so you can immediately identify which source field produced which output. The PO_NUMBER field has been manually changed to 100111 — a value that does not match the constant 100500. The Test Output on the right shows RECIPNT_NO resolved to 100000, confirming the false branch of the condition fired correctly (highlighted by the blue arrow).

The generated payload is fully editable. This is the key point: Generate gives you the structure, and then you adjust individual field values to test specific scenarios without having to author an entire XML document by hand.

Testing the True Branch

Now change PO_NUMBER to 100500 and run the simulation again:

With PO_NUMBER set to 100500, the Test Output immediately shows RECIPNT_NO as 100500. The blue arrow highlights exactly where to confirm the result in the output tree. The condition evaluated true, and the mapping used the matching constant value.

Tips for Using the Simulate Tool Effectively

  • Always test both branches of every condition. It is easy to only verify the happy path — set up values that exercise the false branch too.
  • Test null and empty inputs. Leave a field blank in the test payload and check that your mapping handles it gracefully, especially for fields that feed into script functions.

Summary

Message Mapping in SAP Integration Suite is more capable than it might appear at first glance. The graphical function library handles conditional logic elegantly without a single line of code. Custom Groovy scripts let you express validation, reformatting and derivation logic that the standard nodes cannot cover. And the Simulate tool with its Generate feature gives you a tight test cycle right inside the editor.

To sum up the best-practice: start graphically, reach for a script only when the graphical approach becomes unreadable, and always validate with Simulate before you deploy.

entry.title: MMMFD_PREQ_NAME macros to Validate 'RequisitionerName' field in 'Manage Purchase Order V2'
entry.content:
Hello, 

 

I have a request to modify the field 'Requisitioner name' from a field input to filed input with F4HELP  in both fiori applications for managing PO and PR (Manage Purchase Orders(V2), Manage Purchase Requisitions (Prof)).

With an adaptation project ive succeded to add the F4HELP to the field for both applications.

Now the problem: i have to validate the names that will be written in the field.

My solution: 

- For PR: I implemented the BADI ME_PROCESS_REQ_CUST and in the process item i've used the code below and it's working fine :

                     -In the Footer i have the error message 

                     -The field is in red 
                     -The error message displayed on the field 
Code:
INCLUDE mm_messages_mac.
mmpur_metafield mmmfd_preq_name ." mmmfd_preq_name.
mmpur_message_forced 'E' 'ZSD' '018' '' ' ' ' ' ' '.
im_item->invalidate( ).

-For PO:  I implemented the same logic in the BADI ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST and in the process item methodand i have :

                     -In the Footer i have the error message 

                     -The field not highlighted in red 
                     -The error message not displayed on the field 

 

I tried to find another solution but I haven't succeeded yet.

Please suggest me if you have any idea about this problem or any solution.

Thank you,

Mohamed

entry.title: Re: Missing Sales Order & WBS Fields in Manage Customer L...
entry.content:
Dear @arnap_silea16cc,

In Public Cloud Sales Order Number cannot be added as a supported standard column in Manage Customer Line Items. SAP explicitly states in the app FAQ that sales order details are not displayed in this app. The standard option documented by SAP is to use Original Reference Document to trace the item by the billing document from Sales

For WBS Element and Internal Order the answer is different. If the requirement is to display journal-entry account assignment fields this can be covered in Public Cloud but the supported design is journal entry reporting / drilldown, not extending the standard app with SD sales-order detail. SAP documents that Manage Customer Line Items is extensible through Accounting: Journal Entry Item and SAP also documents standard drilldown to journal entry controlling information including WBS Element

If one consolidated report is required showing customer line item + journal entry + WBS Element + Internal Order + sales-related reference the correct Public Cloud solution is a custom analytical report/query based on C_JournalEntryItemBrowser. SAP positions this CDS view as the released reporting source for journal entry item analysis and it includes fields such as Internal Order, SalesDocument and WBSElementExternalID

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/3b675ddaf93a47f395e64dfbcd2b03b3.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/9d56d8dfe9e34405bb0a16ad8c4aa20b.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/98a0b7ae68b14856acad8228201f77d7.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/d66aded180b44f128cf8a6e7ec19cc96.html

Summary:

No for adding Sales Order Number directly in Manage Customer Line Items.
Yes for covering WBS Element and Internal Order in a supported way but through journal entry drilldown and, if a consolidated output is required, through a custom report on released journal entry CDS content

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Currency Conversion Made Easy with SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
📝Introduction

Hello All,

As we all know that business environment means working with different countries and their currencies but the thing is we need accurate exchange amounts every day. For this I have prepared an Iflow which will convert the Source Currency into Target Currency most accurately calling an external API. I also used HTML to beautify the output.

The below figure shows the Iflow that I have created for the Currency Conversion in SAP Integration Suite:

  •  Here we are sending JSON as input from Postman through HTTPS adapter.
  • Using a GroovyScript to validate the input JSON, if JSON is in correct format the Json will go through the main flow otherwise it will trigger the EXception Subprocess.
  1.  Valid JSON Input:
  • When JSON is in correct format the JSON will be converted into XML using JSON2XML_In
  • CM_Extract_Params(content modifier) is used to extract the parameters like source currency, target currency and the amount from the XML into the exchange properties.
  • Request Reply(RR_Call_API) is used for synchronous communication which will call the exchange rate API  which is an external API service through the HTTP adapter connection.
  •  Will receive JSON as output from external API as response which will be converted into XML payload using JSON2XML_API.
  • This converted XML is used for XSLT mapping(XSLT_HTML) also HTML is used to beautify the output in a tabular format which the target recipient will receive as a mail through mail adapter.

       2. Invalid JSON input:

  • If the input JSON format is failed then the GS_ValidateInput will trigger the Exception Subprocess which will start with Error Start Event.
  • Content modifier with the name CM_Error is used to format the body of the error mail using runtime variables including the Error Category, Flow Name, MPL ID and Error timestamp.
  • Mail adapter in the Exception subprocess will sends the ErrorMail to the support or monitoring team

Working Demo – Input and Output

✔️Successful Case

A valid JSON triggers the flow and returns an email with HTML content showing:

  • Source Currency
  • Target Currency
  • Source Amount
  • Converted Amount

Input from postman for successful Case:

Output/successful currency conversion mail:

❌Error Case

Invalid inputs trigger the Exception Subprocess, such as:

  • Missing fields
  • Incorrect JSON structure
  • External API failure

In such cases, an email is sent containing a clear explanation of the error.

The below image shows the input from postman for the error case where I just removed a comma in the line 3 at target currency which means an invalid JSON input:

Output/Error mail for Invalid Input:

Exception Subprocess Explained:

This part of the flow ensures the system remains stable by handling:

  • Bad input format
  • Missing mandatory fields
  • External API issues

It prevents the main flow from failing silently and provides meaningful alerting via email.

Business Value:

  • This solution delivers business value by delivering business value by automating currency conversion with real time exchange rates.
  • Reduces manual efforts and errors, improves accuracy.
  • Improves supportability by sending proactive error mails with message details like the MPL ID, Iflow name and error details.

🙏Conclusion:

In conclusion this scenario demonstrates how SAP IS can be used for a practical use case involving real time currency conversion.
It’s an excellent example of modernizing business processes using API-driven automation.

@jareenabee_dudekula

entry.title: Offsetting based on Segments or profit centers in SAP GR
entry.content:
Dear expert,

Does the SAP GR public cloud version and private cloud version support offsetting based on Segments or profit centers?

Is it the case that only the private cloud version can support Segment or profit center offsets in the same way as the optimise deployment version?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Offsetting based on Segments or profit centers in SAP GR
entry.content:
Dear Customer, 

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Group Reporting, offsetting and elimination logic is based on the standard, predefined consolidation data model (ACDOCU).

  • Dimension Constraints:
    In the Public Cloud edition, Segment and Profit Center are not part of the consolidation data model used for Group Reporting.

  • Elimination Logic:
    As these dimensions are not included in the consolidation data model, they are not available for use as drivers in elimination logic or offsetting within the Consolidation Monitor or in the “Manage Rules for Automatic Postings” application.

  • Functional Scope:
    Offsetting in Public Cloud is performed using standard dimensions such as Consolidation Unit, Partner Unit, FS Item, and Subitem.

  • Deployment Differences:
    In contrast, such requirements can be implemented in Private Cloud or On-Premise environments, where the data model allows for greater extensibility.

For more details and further guidence, please refer to the SAP Help Documents:

Group Reporting | SAP Help Portal

Consolidation Master Data Fields | SAP Help Portal

2659656 - FAQ About SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Group Reporting - SAP for Me

entry.title: Re: Inconsistent Billed Revenue Display Across Event-Base...
entry.content:
Dear @arnap_silea16cc,

In Public Cloud this should be treated as standard system behavior. SAP documents Event-Based Revenue Recognition with separate key figures such as Billed Revenue, Sales Deduction/Surcharge, Revenue Adjustment, and Recognized Revenue where Recognized Revenue = Billed Revenue + Sales Deduction/Surcharge + Revenue Adjustment. This confirms that the system processes these components separately but SAP does not document a delivered standard field called Net Invoiced Amount that is shown consistently across the standard project apps. Accordingly in Event-Based Revenue Recognition Projects and in its period view the absence of a standalone adjusted/net invoiced amount should be considered standard delivered behawior

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/abe963495b29415289023ee55759a89d.html

For Project Profitability the key distinction is that the underlying analytical content does contain the relevant measures, including Billed Revenue, Sales Deduction, Revenue Adjustment, and Recognized Revenue. This means the limitation is not the absence of the data in the Public Cloud analytical model but the fact that the delivered standard apps do not expose the same measures in the same layout across all reporting views

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/9876fd869cf84049998fb6601e90eda5.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/8c8defb9b926469899f1896d99e6398b.html

The supported approach in Public Cloud is therefore custom analytics, not CBC/SSCUI configuration of the delivered apps. SAP documents that Custom Analytical Queries support calculated measures, so the standard workaround is to create a business-specific KPI such as a net invoiced amount derived from billed revenue and sales deduction/surcharge. From a reporting design perspective the recommended solution is to build a custom reporting layer based on released analytical content. A practical starting point is Enterprise Project Margin Cube for project revenue and margin measures, Billing Document Pricing Element for billing pricing components and Sales Volume / Pricing Elements Flexible Analysis where billing-based invoiced values and invoice references are required. SAP documents Sales Volume as invoiced sales analysis and Pricing Elements Flexible Analysis as the view showing which pricing elements increase or reduce sales volume

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/33f3a229ecfe438ca6b2053c48b4e1bb.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/4ef099d254cd4f8a96df001496f53952.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/b8dd49e489f347c4b3ff88c4793fd27a.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/cc4f03e7e20a4542bd8c19e9b70ae039.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/9876fd869cf84049998fb6601e90eda5.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/5993a257bbd54621929b8a1fbfcb321e.htm

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/b1853554e448cb7de10000000a44176d.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/43a7db03846744369b07e4443d6a8827.html

Summary:

This is standard SAP Public Cloud behavior the delivered apps do not consistently show a standalone net invoiced amount across project revenue views. The recommended and supported solution is to use custom analytics on released analytical content and add the required calculated measure so the business has one consistent figure across reporting

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Make Company Code Settings - Asset Accounting-Specific
entry.content:
Dear @arul_selvan191,

In Public Cloud FY2026 cannot be opened in Asset Accounting while FY2024 is still open. SAP states that only two fiscal years can be open at the same time in Asset Accounting. As a result to open FY2026, FY2024 must first be closed

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/290e97c27ba3416cb7254cabe4d5dec4.html

This is standard system behavior, not a configuration defect. SAP documents Make Company Code Settings Asset Accounting-Specific (F7312) as the app used for year-end closing for individual depreciation areas. Therefore for a productive company code, FY2024 must be closed before FY2026 can be opened. If depreciation has been managed outside SAP the only SAP-supported alternative is a legacy data transfer / migration scenario. SAP documents that depreciation already posted in the legacy system can be transferred into SAP instead of being newly posted there. However, SAP’s migration guidance is based on the last closed fiscal year and does not support keeping FY2024 open while opening FY2026 in normal operations

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/cb476007e11c4221b22d87b80ed18c6d.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/1fd2035773b15632e10000000a441470.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/a185c3d89dae4ffc85723f27f624944a.html

Summary:

The system message is consistent with SAP standard. For a productive Asset Accounting company code, FY2024 must be closed first external depreciation handling changes the design only in a legacy transfer scenario and does not remove the fiscal-year opening control

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: How to get cart item's grouped line number with a script?

When the "Roll-Up Cart Items" AppParam is enabled, cart line item numbers are displayed as nested.

For example, if items 1, 4, 6 in the below list are main products,
1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
then enabling that AppParam changes them to:
1, 1.1, 1.2, 2, 2.1, 3

But the script Quote.MainItems.Item.Rank returns the flat line item number.
What script can I use to get the nested format?

entry.title: System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA: Downtime Reduction with Technical Options
entry.content:
Document Teaser 

Downtime is always a topic in most SAP S/4HANA conversion projects. From technical point of view, what can we do to reduce and optimize the downtime? Let me share my experiences with this topic as part of my work in the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team. 

1.   SAP S/4HANA Overview of Transition scenarios 

The transition to SAP S/4HANA is a strategic business transformation that involves moving from legacy systems like SAP ECC to SAP's modern, in-memory ERP platform. This transition is essential since SAP is planning to end standard support for SAP ECC in December 2027, with extended maintenance available until 2030. 

You may know the SAP S/4HANA overview of transition scenarios below. This blog will focus on SAP S/4HANA conversion scenarios only. 

2.   SAP S/4HANA Standard Conversion General Steps in downtime windows 

In a standard SAP S/4HANA conversion, the business downtime window is the period from when the system is unavailable for end users until it will become available for them again. 

Let’s look at the following general phases: 

  • System Ramp Down and Isolation: The process begins mainly with locking all users and stopping interfaces from/to external systems to ensure that no new data will be entered. Those are rather customer-specific steps, please always have your conversion delivery team align in detail with the customer IT and business teams. 
  • Software Update Manager (SUM) Downtime: SUM finalizes the conversion of SAP S/4HANA and switches the system to the new repository, this is the main part from technical point of view which we can optimize to reduce the total downtime. 
  • Finance (FI) Data Conversion: SUM converts FI table contents from the old SAP ERP data model to the new SAP S/4HANA data model. We treat it as a candidate for optimization, but we will not go into detail about it in this blog. 
  • SAP S/4HANA Technical and Application post-processing steps: Those steps are critical to finalize the system to get it ready again for business operations. These steps ensure that all functional requirements are covered with the new S/4HANA data models and that the system's technical infrastructure is stable. .System Validation and Ramp-up: Technical teams and key functional users verify core configurations and critical processes before releasing the system back to the end users. 

3.    Terminology and Approaches 

3.1   SAP S/4HANA Conversion Tools and Approaches (Conversion only) 

Approaches 

Scenarios and how to reduce the downtime 

Standard SUM/DMO 

Software Update Manager/Database Migration Option - Conversion with DB migration 

Depending on whether the source system is on an SAP HANA database or not, we will choose to use SUM or DMO by picking up different endpoints to launch for the conversion. There are general recommendations to work on to reduce the technical tool downtime periods. 

doDMO (Downtime Optimized Approach) 

Downtime-optimized Database Migration Option - Conversion with DB migration and downtime reduction (DB migration partly during uptime) 

doC (Downtime Optimized Approach) 

Downtime-optimized Conversion - Conversion with significant downtime reduction (DB migration partly and data conversion partly during uptime) 

DMO with System Move 

Conversion with DB migration and data center relocation 

DMOVE2S4  

DMO Move to SAP S/4HANA - Conversion with DB migration and data center relocation 

DMOVE2S4 with doDMO 

Combining DMOVE2S4 and doDMO – DC move, S/4HANA Conversion and reduce downtime by DB migration partly during uptime 

DMOVE2S4 with doC 

Combine DMOVE2S4 and doC  – DC move, S/4HANA Conversion and reduce downtime by DB migration and data conversion partly during uptime 

System Transition Workbench 

A guided tool for moving to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition includeing different scenarios 

NZDM 

near-Zero Downtime Maintenance (ABAP)  - Conversion from SAP Business Suite on HANA to SAP S/4HANA,  an advanced preconfiguration mode and feature in SUM with Record and Replay technique to reduce downtime   

NZDT 

Near-Zero Downtime Technology 

3.2 How to choose the most suitable conversion strategy and conversion path  

Scenario 

Source System 

Available Options 

On-Premise In-Place Conversion 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on non-HANA Database 

DMO, doDMO, doC 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on HANA Database 

SUM, nZDM, doDMO, doC 

Migration & Conversion to Hyperscalers / Customer's Other Data Center 

SAP ERP 6.0 on non-HANA Database 

DMO with System Move, DMOVE2S4, DMOVE2S4 with doDMO, DMOVE2S4 with doC 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on HANA Database 

DMO with System Move, DMOVE2S4, DMOVE2S4 with doDMO, DMOVE2S4 with doC 

Migration & Conversion to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on non-HANA Database 

DMO with System Move, DMOVE2S4, DMOVE2S4 with doDMO, DMOVE2S4 with doC, System Transition Workbench 

 

SAP ERP 6.0 on HANA Database 

DMO with System Move, DMOVE2S4, DMOVE2S4 with doDMO, DMOVE2S4 with doC, System Transition Workbench 

What are the factors and criteria for the decision making with different options?  

From technical point of view, it is not a complicated question, see below decision tree. 

At the same time, you can refer to Road Map Viewer - SAP for Me for the general information 

From overall project perspective, there are some important factors such as: 

  • Total project timeline and cost; 
  • Downtime reduction; 
  • Technical prerequisites and functional prerequisites; 
  • Etc. and customer can define by themselves. Here is a delivery example about the evaluation of the approach and tools options with pros and cons based on customer’s requirements and SAP shared the proposal in a high-level picture. 

If still you have doubts, please reach out to your SAP professional consulting and service teams about additional recommendations for the different approaches based on the system data, sizing report, readiness reports and so onSome customers we worked with choose in-place SAP S/4HANA conversion in their data center and migration of SAP S/4HANA with Copy to target SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. If you choose this option, please consider the total business downtime. And right now, SAP provides this with featured option: RISE with System Transition Workbench with scenario: SUM/DMO run in local data center and use HANA tenant copy to achieve this. 

 3.3   RISE with SAP System Transition Workbench  

It provides a pre-planned “route” tailored to your starting point and destination, saving you the time to plan and reach the route. It gives real-time traffic updates and suggests alternative routes, helping you avoid congestion and delays. 

About "RISE with SAP system transition workbench" | SAP Help Portal  

SAP System Transition Workbench is installed by Enterprise Cloud Services(ECS) on a VM Migration Server. You can get seamless support from your SAP delivery team and SAP development teams as it is released and generally available to all customers. 

There is one great introduction blog for this tool from Kristin. 

Introducing the RISE with SAP System Transition Wo... - SAP CommunityThe tool can be used for migration of customer systems to SAP Enterprise Cloud Services (ECS) in context of RISE with SAP. 

  • SAP HANA Tenant Copy 
  • SAP HANA Backup and Recovery 
  • Homogeneous "Update and Copy" 
  • System Conversion using SUM 2.0 (DMO with System Move) 
  • System Conversion using SUM 2.0 (DMOVE2S4) 

Usage Restriction 

  • The above mentioned scenario is permitted for SAP ECS internal operations as well as for qualified migration partners. 
    Qualified migration partners must have earned at least the “Essential Level” designation for the SAP Cloud ERP Private competency as visible in SAP Partner Finder. 
  • SAP provides required automation tooling and configuration of the virtual machine for the migration.  
    Any non-authorized software export of the automation tooling or its configuration is prohibited. 
    Any usage beyond defined usage constraints is not allowed. 
  • The RISE with SAP System Transition Workbench assumes that the target system and component releases are within SAP maintenance. Technical limitations and/or (general) restrictions might require additional preparation or an upgrade to newer releases of SAP S/4HANA related applications, add-ons, or other technical components. 
  • Currently, the RISE with SAP system transition workbench does not support SAP HANA scale-out target systems 

There is also the published success story of RISE with System Transition workbench helping from Piyush and Praveen to customer accelerated on their cloud journey to RISE.   

Accelerating RISE Migrations: CommScope’s Success Story with Transition Workbench Update and Copy - An Innovative migration approach for Rise 

3.4   Near-Zero Downtime Technology 

NZDT and doC are the two approaches as part of Minimized Downtime Services supporting all scenarios.  

As per SAP Note 693168 - Minimized Downtime Service (MDS) , doC is generally available to customers while NZDT is service-based and SAP Global Delivery Hub would have to be involved. Please always reach out to your SAP consulting team about NZDT scenarios since only a  limited number of customers can be supported. 

4.   Second level considerations and SUM/DMO optimizations 

4.1    Technical Downtime Optimization (TDO) app  

 To ensure that the maintenance using Software Update Manager on the production system goes smoothly, test cycles are performed before updating  production. And typically, the updated system is used to validate the upgrade process and identify any necessary preparation steps and fixes. 

To address these concerns, SAP offer the Technical Downtime Optimization App. This app provides multiple features for analyzing the Software Update Manager runtime and improving the maintenance event. 

 

You can refer to more details about the TDO usage: https://tdo.cloud.sap 

SAP Blog : Simply analyzing and improving maintenance events with Technical Downtime Optimization App 

4.2   General Recommendations to reduce technical downtime 

SAP Note 2351294 - S/4HANA System Conversion / Upgrade: Measures to reduce technical downtime 

4.3   General Recommendations on SAP HANA Database 

  • SAP HANA parameter changes will help data conversion performance but may impact productive performance. Therefore, if you apply these changes before entering the technical downtime, don’t forget to revert those changes after the completion of SUM. 
  • Increase the CPU capacity used for delta merges by changing the indexserver.ini parameter [mergedog] -> load_balancing_func value to “LCC / 2”. Remember the original value to restore it later. 
  • Increase the number of merge tokens and merge threads by setting indexserver.ini parameters [mergedog] -> token_per_table and [indexing] -> parallel_merge_threads to a value of available physical CPU cores divided by 2 (but no more than 256). 
  • Increase the parallelism for partitioned tables by setting indexserver.ini parameter [indexing] -> parallel_merge_part_threads to a value of available physical CPU cores divided by 2 (but no more than 256). If the parameter is not yet available in section [indexing], add it as a new parameter. 
  • Please monitor the memory usage of the HANA database server via visual load history of the HANA cockpit, or the relevant monitoring during the execution of critical phases. 

4.4   General recommendations on SUM/DMO parameters 

Setting up parallel processes properly can dramatically increase the performance of upgrades and updates of SAP systems. As best practice, the following should be considered: 

  • SAP Note 1616401 - Parallelism in the Upgrades, EhPs and Support Packages implementations 
  • Hardware resource utilization during Upgrades and Updates 

              SUM settings 1: ABAP PROCESSES (UPTIME) and ABAP PROCESSES (DOWNTIME) 

Number of parallel background processes and activator processes, also number of batch processes scheduled on the application server. The minimum number is 5. We recommend a value that is high enough to enable parallel processing, but small enough that the production system is not affected. 

              SUM settings 2: SQL PROCESSES (UPTIME) and SQL PROCESES (DOWNTIME) 

Number of parallel SQL processes used by tp to execute database statements. 

              SUM settings 3: R3TRANS PROCESSES (UPTIME) and R3TRANS PROCESSES (DOWNTIME) 

Number of parallel import processes used by R3trans to import data into the database. Note that these processes can generate significant loads on the database. 

              SUM settings 4: R3LOAD PROCESSES 

Number of R3load processes used for the EU_IMPORT phases. 

 

PHASES (SUM)

PARALLELISM MECHANISM

ACT_UPG

BATCH

DDIC_UPG

R3TRANS

PARCONV_UPG

SQL DDL

PARDIST_SHD

BATCH

SHADOW_IMPORT_INC

R3TRANS

SHADOW_IMPORT_UPG1

R3TRANS

SHADOW_IMPORT_UPG2

R3TRANS

TABIM_UPG

R3TRANS

XPRAS_AIMMRG

BATCH

EU_CLONE_MIG_DT/UT_RUN

R3load

 

4.5  The classic optimization methods 

  • Usually optimization is started with analysis of files SAPupConsole.log, LONGPOST LOG, UPGANA.XML and APPLANA.XML. 
  • DMO optimization: Benchmark tool for export/import: see blog Optimizing DMO Performance - SAP Community. 
  • Parallelization framework (SHDB_PFW_CONF) - based on hardware resources of the application server. 
  • SAP Note 2353814 - SAP S/4HANA conversion reports in SD: optimize parallel execution of conversion reports: . 
  • For AnyDB to SAP S/4HANA conversion: 
  • Duration files can be re-used and R3load migration logs are analyzed to check if better splitting can be determined, or a different order of packages. 
  • Source database statistics updates and, in some cases index rebuild as well as table reorganization can reduce the runtime. 

4.6    The steps and phases that contribute most during SUM/DMO downtime: 

  

Phase #  

Key Technical Steps 

Description 

1 

EU_CLONE_MIG_DT_EXP / IMP 

Export and Import: Application tables are migrated from the source database to the target HANA database. 

2 

PARCONV_UPG/ 

PARMVNT_APP_VIEWS 

Parallel Conversion: Converts application tables to the new release's structure and activates their nametabs. 

3 

TABIM_UPG 

Main Table Import: Imports remaining data into SAP-standard tables (classes S, W, E, C, G). 

4 

XPRAS_AIMMRG 

Execution of XPRAs: Runs Reports After Import (XPRAs) to adjust data and settings for the new system version. - Data conversion 

5 

JOB_KONV_MIGRATION/ 

RUN_VBFA_MIGRATION 

SFW - Shadow field Migration for KONV-PRCD_ELEMENTS Conversion 

6 

PARRUN_MKPF*_S4 / 

PARRUN_MKPF_VERIFY 

MM-IM conversion 

7 

PARRUN_FILL_EXTRACT_S4 

MM-IM Conversion 

8 

XPRAS_AIMMRG 

Execution of XPRAs: Runs Reports After Import (XPRAs) to adjust data and settings for the new system version. 

9 

Customer-specific XPRAs 

TABIM_UPG_CUST 

XPRAS_UPG_CUST 

Customer-specific XPRAs 

10 

RUN_SDMI_POST_PROCESSING 

Silent Data Migration: A post-processing step that migrates application data in the background after the system is back online, reducing technical downtime. 

4.7   PFW (SHDB Parallelization Framework) settings based on hardware resources of the App server 

SHDB Parallelization Framework  is and ABAP SAP_BASIS framework to simplify the parallelization topic for ABAP applications via PFW instances covering downtime conversion steps.              

The configuration table SHDB_PFW_CONF  

Report/ t_code:: SHDB_PDW_SUPPORT to configure and adjust the parameters or via scripts directly from sap note 2351294 - S/4HANA System Conversion / Upgrade: Measures to reduce technical downtime 

If still you have doubts, please reach out to your SAP professional consulting and service teams about additional recommendations for the different approaches based on the system data, sizing report, readiness reports and so on. 

5. Summary and Conclusion 

Optimizing downtime for an SAP S/4HANA conversion is not just about choosing the right tool; it is about a multi-layered approach that combines strategy, infrastructure tuning, and precise tool configuration. 

By selecting the most appropriate transition scenario (Standard, doDMO, or doC), leveraging the Technical Downtime Optimization (TDO) app, and fine-tuning parallelism within SUM and the HANA database, you can significantly shrink the business impact of your cutover. 

Key Takeaways: 

  • Analyze Early: Use the TDO app and previous mock run logs to identify bottlenecks. 
  • Tune the Engine: Don't settle for default SUM parameters; adjust ABAP and SQL processes based on your hardware. 
  • Leverage Modern Tools: Consider and compare the tools accelerate the journey. 

Ultimately, every system is unique. We highly recommend performing multiple test conversions to validate these optimizations and ensure a smooth, predictable go-live. 

If you have other questions / feedback, please let me know in the comments. 

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team.

entry.title: Re: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Migration Assessment Agent (Part 2 of 6)
entry.content: @atul_joshi85 Thank you, that's exactly the balance I was aiming for. Clean core and AI aren't in tension when the agent operates as an assessment and recommendation layer rather than touching the core directly. The agent queries, analyzes, and surfaces findings — the human and the governed process own the action. That separation is what makes it both safe and practical for production SAP landscapes. Glad it resonated!

Before you move your stories in SAC, just head to the Bookmarks tab in the Stories area to clear things out. It’s the easiest way to delete them all in one spot!
